symbols: Right hand raised in blessing — Christ bestowing grace, the two fingers representing the two natures (divine and human)
Red Gospel book in left hand — the Word of God, the Book of Life
Gold halo — the divine glory of the Son of God
IC XC monogram — Greek abbreviation for Jesus Christ
Gold background — the eternal, transcendent realm
description: A traditional Orthodox icon depicts Christ in half-length frontal view against a gold background. He has shoulder-length brown hair, a short beard, and a large golden halo. He wears a dark blue-black outer garment over a red-orange inner robe. His right hand is raised with fingers arranged in a blessing gesture. His left hand holds a red Gospel book with an ornate jeweled cover. The letters IC and XC appear in red circles at the upper corners. The icon shows signs of age with surface wear.
theological_description: The Christ Pantocrator ('Almighty' or 'Ruler of All') is the quintessential Orthodox icon, combining Christ's roles as Teacher, Judge, and Savior in a single image. The blessing hand declares: 'Peace I leave with you; my peace I give to you' (JHN.14.27). The Gospel book affirms Christ as the Word made flesh: 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God' (JHN.1.1). The dual nature of Christ — fully God and fully human — is conveyed through the two different halves of His face in traditional iconography: one side showing gentleness and mercy, the other showing severity and judgment. Colossians declares: 'He is the image (eikon) of the invisible God' (COL.1.15) — making the icon itself a theological statement about the possibility of depicting God through the Incarnation.

scripture_refs.reason: He is the image of the invisible God — the theological basis for depicting Christ in icons
In the beginning was the Word — Christ holds the Gospel as the incarnate Logos
Peace I give to you — the blessing gesture of Christ's right hand
I am the Alpha and Omega, says the Lord God, the Almighty — the Pantocrator
When the Son of Man comes in glory — the dual role of Judge and Savior in the Pantocrator face
